Metadata.Coram: Wong Sung Boon v Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd and another
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Wong Sung Boon

Facts.Text: From 2011-04-01, Wong Sung Boon held the positions of Senior Managing Director and Chief Executive Officer of Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd.
Wong's appointment as Senior Managing Director was governed by a Letter of Appointment dated 2016-04-01, described as his Employment Contract.
The Employment Contract for Wong as Senior Managing Director was renewed with an employment period from 2017-04-01 to 2018-03-31.

A media release in 2014-04 stated that Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd would offer one-stop financial solutions for businesses, including hire purchase or leasing, sales and leaseback schemes, credit control outsourcing services, litigation services and other financial solutions.

Around 2014-04, public materials described Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd and its Innovation Office in Singapore as operating a consultancy business, building analytics, and expanding into wearable technology, telepresence, artificial intelligence and a cloud-based audio tour guide service.

Around 2001 to 2002, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd began offering project management services involving planning projects, sourcing and liaising with suppliers, structuring contracts, coordinating with suppliers and customers, and monitoring milestones.

Under his terms of appointment as Managing Director and later Senior Managing Director, Wong was devote his best efforts to promote, develop and extend the business of Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd in Singapore.

In 2017-09, Deloitte & Touche Financial Advisory Services Pte Ltd was commissioned by Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd to conduct an audit investigation into certain past transactions, with the work take place between 2017-09-13 and 2017-09-29.
On 2017-10-19, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd suspended Wong from his role with full pay pending the outcome of the audit investigation.

On 2017-11-08, Deloitte & Touche Financial Advisory Services Pte Ltd issued a report of its audit investigation concerning past transactions of Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd involving I-Connect Interactive Pte Ltd and Supreme Lion Holding Pte Ltd.

On 2017-12-07, Fujifilm's Global Audit Division produced a special audit report highlighting irregularities in Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd's transactions with I-Connect Interactive Pte Ltd, Supreme Lion Holding Pte Ltd and M.O.S. Marine Offshore Services Pte Ltd.
On 2017-10-30, Deloitte & Touche Financial Advisory Services Pte Ltd held a teleconference with Wong as part of its audit investigation.
On 2017-11-30, Wong attended a meeting with Deloitte & Touche Financial Advisory Services Pte Ltd in connection with the audit investigation.

On 2011-10-11, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd and I-Connect Interactive Pte Ltd entered into a Master Services Agreement for Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd to supply, construct, install and commission base transceiver station equipment and facilities for a satellite infrastructure project in Indonesia for approximately SGD 4.3 million, referred to as the IC1 Contract.

On 2012-03-16, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd and I-Connect Interactive Pte Ltd entered into a Master Services Agreement for Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd to supply, construct and install fibre optic infrastructure for a satellite infrastructure project in Indonesia for approximately SGD 5.59 million, referred to as the IC2 Contract.
By 2011-11-01, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd's Legal Department had signed the IC1 Contract with I-Connect Interactive Pte Ltd after internal clearance processes.

Around 2012-04-01, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd and I-Connect Interactive Pte Ltd completed internal credit evaluation and executed the IC2 Contract after relevant departments, including the Legal Department, had cleared the contract.
On 2011-10-20, a director of I-Connect Interactive Pte Ltd, Raden, signed the IC1 Contract with Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd.

In early 2012, during a Business Review Meeting, Wong informed the then President of Asia Pacific Operations and President of Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd, Katsuhiko Yanagawa, that Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd had entered into the IC1 Contract and intended to enter into the IC2 Contract.

By mid-2013, I-Connect Interactive Pte Ltd had an outstanding amount of SGD 557,921.50 payable to Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd under the IC2 Contract, which was treated internally as a debt referred to as the IC Debt.
Around 2014-06, Wong approved the internal accounting treatment to write off the outstanding IC Debt of SGD 557,921.50 owed by I-Connect Interactive Pte Ltd to Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd.

In 2015, at the annual general meeting of Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd to approve its audited accounts for the financial year April 2014 to March 2015, the accounts presented to the board and shareholders reflected the IC Debt as written off.

On 2014-03-05, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd and M.O.S. Marine Offshore Services Pte Ltd entered into a sales agreement for Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd to supply offshore diving equipment and provide testing, commissioning and installation services for SGD 1,000,000, referred to as the MOS1 Contract.

On 2014-12-01, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd and M.O.S. Marine Offshore Services Pte Ltd entered into a sales agreement for Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd to sell diving equipment and repair, refurbish and test a transformer and capacitor load bank for SGD 2,000,000, referred to as the MOS2 Contract.

On 2015-08-28, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd and M.O.S. Marine Offshore Services Pte Ltd entered into a rental agreement for Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd to lease diving equipment and provide testing, commissioning and installation services for SGD 1,000,000, referred to as the MOS3 Contract.

On 2015-11-04, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd and M.O.S. Marine Offshore Services Pte Ltd entered into a rental agreement for Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd to lease diving equipment and provide repair and reconditioning work on a transformer and capacitor load bank unit for SGD 1,007,496, referred to as the MOS4 Contract.

In early 2014, during a Business Review Meeting, Wong informed the then President of Asia Pacific Operations, Masashi Honda, that Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd was considering transactions with M.O.S. Marine Offshore Services Pte Ltd involving diving equipment.

On 2014-12-19, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd issued a purchase order to Serve Tech Container Specialist Pte Ltd for fabrication and installation works of diving equipment related to the MOS2 Contract, referencing standard terms and conditions on the reverse or on a website.

On 2013-12-06, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd entered into a sale agreement with Supreme Lion Holding Pte Ltd to supply products and services for installation, testing and commissioning of power control units for woodwork machinery systems.

On 2013-12-19, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd issued a purchase order to Hyper Marketing (Singapore) Pte Ltd for woodwork machinery system products and services related to the Supreme Lion transaction, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd's standard purchase order terms and conditions.
By 2014-01-01, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd had implemented an internal Abacus billing system that required contract descriptions to be selected from predefined categories such as rental agreements.
On 2011-10-19, a credit evaluation form for the IC1 Contract with I-Connect Interactive Pte Ltd was submitted within Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd's credit evaluation process.
On 2012-03-29, a credit evaluation form and related credit evaluation documents for the IC2 Contract with I-Connect Interactive Pte Ltd were completed within Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd.

By 2014-01-01, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd's credit evaluation process involved credit evaluation forms, scorecards and reports, and could use internal records and publicly available information when listed supporting documents such as recent bank statements or audited financial statements were not available.

In 2015, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd formalised certain operating procedures in an Operating Procedure Manual, which did not specify a mandatory requirement to obtain personal guarantees for transactions.

On 2011-10-01, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd had in place a 2006 Communication Matrix that required concurrence from the President of Asia Pacific Operations for certain categories of business activities, including business activities outside the territory and write-off of bad debts above specified thresholds.

On 2013-12-02, an email was sent to various persons including Wong attaching the 2013 Communication Matrix, which set out approval requirements for new business activities and write-off of debts above specified amounts.

By 2014-12-01, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd's credit evaluation documentation for the MOS2 Contract recorded that the company would finance construction materials for diving equipment with a financed amount of SGD 1,000,000 at an interest rate of 3.5% and monthly payments over 12 months.

Around 2014-12-01, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd prepared a manual costing sheet for the MOS2 Contract showing an internal cost of SGD 1,930,000 for goods and services from suppliers and total revenue of SGD 2,000,000, reflecting a gross profit of SGD 70,000.

Around 2015-08-28, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd prepared a manual costing sheet for the MOS3 Contract showing an internal cost of approximately SGD 965,000 and total contract revenue of SGD 1,000,000.

Around 2015-11-04, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd prepared a manual costing sheet for the MOS4 Contract showing an internal cost of approximately SGD 955,480 and total contract revenue of SGD 1,007,496.

For the MOS2 Contract, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd obtained personal guarantees from three individuals associated with M.O.S. Marine Offshore Services Pte Ltd: director and shareholder Samuel Yeo, and individuals Rachael Yeo and Elizabeth Lee.

For the MOS3 and MOS4 Contracts, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd obtained personal guarantees from four individuals associated with M.O.S. Marine Offshore Services Pte Ltd: Samuel Yeo, Rachael Yeo, Elizabeth Lee and Adrian Lim.

In 2013-08, a bankruptcy application was filed against Suleman, one of the personal guarantors for I-Connect Interactive Pte Ltd, as reflected in a search conducted after letters of demand were sent regarding the IC Debt.
By mid-2014, internal searches showed that one of the personal guarantors for I-Connect Interactive Pte Ltd, Raden, was working as a taxi driver at that time.
Credit evaluation documents for the MOS2 Contract recorded that the combined annual income of personal guarantors Samuel and Rachael Yeo was approximately SGD 675,000 based on income tax assessments.

Credit evaluation documents for the MOS4 Contract recorded that the combined annual income of personal guarantors Samuel and Rachael Yeo was approximately SGD 1,460,000 based on updated income tax assessments.
By late 2014, M.O.S. Marine Offshore Services Pte Ltd had paid more than 96% of the total contract price under the MOS1 Contract to Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd.

By the time the MOS3 Contract was entered into on 2015-08-28, M.O.S. Marine Offshore Services Pte Ltd had paid approximately 70% of the contract price under the MOS2 Contract to Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd.

By the time the MOS4 Contract was entered into on 2015-11-04, M.O.S. Marine Offshore Services Pte Ltd had fully paid the MOS1 Contract and had paid approximately 40% of the contract price under the MOS3 Contract to Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd.
By 2016-01-01, M.O.S. Marine Offshore Services Pte Ltd had outstanding payment obligations to Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd under the MOS Contracts amounting to approximately SGD 2,322,464.28.
Under the sale agreement dated 2013-12-06, Supreme Lion Holding Pte Ltd paid Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd the agreed contract price for the woodwork machinery system products and services supplied.

On 2017-12-21, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd issued a termination notice to Wong stating that his employment was terminated with immediate effect pursuant to specified clauses of his Employment Contract and that he would not receive notice pay or an end of term payment.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based only on pre-dispute conduct and contractual arrangements, Wong's actions in relation to the I-Connect, MOS and Supreme Lion transactions and internal approval and credit processes correspond to a lawful summary termination of his employment or to an unlawful dismissal with resulting contractual entitlements such as notice pay, end-of-term payment, bonus and leave salary.
Party_Details.Rule: Under Singapore employment and company law, an employer may summarily terminate an employee's fixed-term contract only where the employee's conduct falls within contractually specified grounds such as gross default or misconduct, material damage caused intentionally or through negligence, or conduct grossly incompatible with the due and faithful discharge of duties. Directors and senior managers are exercise reasonable care, skill and diligence comparable to a reasonable person in their position, act in good faith in the interests of the company, comply with internal approval matrices and procedures, and manage credit and transactional risk through documented processes. Where a summary termination is not justified, damages are generally assessed by reference to the minimum legal obligation, limiting recovery to the period and payments the employer would have been bound to provide if it had exercised its contractual power to terminate in the least costly lawful manner, including salary in lieu of notice and other contractual payments that would have accrued within that period.
Party_Details.Application: Wong held senior executive roles under a fixed-term Employment Contract that expressly allowed Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd to terminate his employment by giving three months' basic salary in lieu of notice and also contained specific summary termination clauses tied to categories of conduct. During his tenure, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd expanded into project management and third-party product transactions, and internal documents and public materials described a broad business scope including financial solutions and consultancy-type services. The company operated under Communication Matrices that required concurrence from the President of Asia Pacific Operations for certain business activities and for write-offs above defined thresholds, and under a credit evaluation framework that used forms, scorecards and reports and did not impose a mandatory requirement for personal guarantees. Wong was involved in causing Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd to enter into the IC1 and IC2 Contracts with I-Connect Interactive Pte Ltd, the MOS1–MOS4 Contracts with M.O.S. Marine Offshore Services Pte Ltd, and the Supreme Lion transaction, with internal legal and finance staff participating in contract clearance and credit evaluation. For the I-Connect transactions, personal guarantees were obtained and the IC Debt was later written off after being reflected in audited accounts presented to and signed off by the board and the then President of Asia Pacific Operations. For the MOS transactions, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd contracted directly with suppliers, obtained multiple personal guarantees from individuals associated with M.O.S. Marine Offshore Services Pte Ltd, and documented internal costing and revenue recognition consistent with sale or rental structures rather than stand-alone cash loans. For the Supreme Lion transaction, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd contracted with a supplier under purchase orders incorporating standard terms and conditions, including warranties and indemnities, and the customer paid the agreed price. These pre-dispute facts Wong operated within an established internal framework that included legal review, credit evaluation and senior regional oversight, and that the company had mechanisms to regularise approvals and write-offs. When the audit reports were issued in late 2017, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd chose to suspend Wong and then issue a termination notice on 2017-12-21 invoking summary termination clauses and denying notice pay and end-of-term payment. Given the existence of a contractual notice mechanism and the ability to terminate at the end of the fixed term, the minimum legal obligation framework focuses on what the employer could and would have done lawfully at that time, namely to terminate by paying three months' basic salary in lieu of notice and to address any transactional issues through internal governance and potential civil claims rather than by withholding contractual termination benefits.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The court analysed the structure and scope of Wong's Employment Contract, including clause 8.1 on termination by three months' basic salary in lieu of notice and clause 10 on summary dismissal for specified categories of conduct such as causing material damage, gross default or misconduct, and conduct grossly incompatible with the due and faithful discharge of duties. It examined the factual matrix of the I-Connect, MOS and Supreme Lion transactions, the Deloitte Report and the Fujifilm Global Audit Division special audit report, and the internal Communication Matrices and credit evaluation processes. For the I-Connect transactions, the court found that the IC1 and IC2 Contracts were project management-type arrangements with back-to-back supplier contracts, that the Tsalasa Contract contained warranties and undertakings protecting Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd, and that there was no evidence of I-Connect alleging breach or of suppliers failing to perform. The court accepted evidence that Wong consulted relevant departments, that the Legal Department cleared the IC1 Contract, and that the then President of Asia Pacific Operations, Yanagawa, orally approved the IC1 and IC2 Contracts at a Business Review Meeting, thereby satisfying or curing any Communication Matrix approval requirement. The court held that although Wong did not initially obtain formal approval before entering the IC1 Contract or before writing off the IC Debt, subsequent approval by the regional president and the board, including Honda's acceptance of the audited accounts reflecting the write-off, rectified any procedural breach. It concluded that these matters did not cause material damage to Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd and did not amount to conduct grossly incompatible with Wong's duties or to gross default or misconduct under clause 10(b), (h) or (i).

For the MOS transactions, the court rejected the defendants' characterisation of the MOS2, MOS3 and MOS4 Contracts as pure loans disguised as sales or rentals. It relied on the contractual documents, supplier quotations and purchase orders, manual costing sheets, and testimony from finance staff to find that the MOS2 Contract was an instalment sale with a gross profit margin and that the MOS3 and MOS4 Contracts, though documented as rental agreements due to limitations of the Abacus billing system, were in substance asset sale or lease transactions with underlying goods and services supplied by Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd through its suppliers. The court noted that Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd contracted directly with suppliers, that goods were delivered and no complaints were raised by MOS, and that the Legal Department had cleared the MOS2 Contract. It accepted that Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd was already providing commercial financing and project management services and that the MOS transactions fell within an expanded business model encouraged by the regional management. The court found that Wong obtained multiple personal guarantees from individuals associated with MOS, that detailed credit evaluations were performed, and that MOS had a positive payment history before later default. It held that the defendants failed to show that Wong exposed the company to unreasonable or unnecessary liability or that he failed to exercise reasonable diligence.

Regarding the Supreme Lion transaction, the court found that Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd entered into a sale agreement to supply woodwork machinery system products and services, that it contracted with Hyper Marketing (Singapore) Pte Ltd under purchase orders incorporating standard terms, warranties and indemnities, and that Supreme Lion paid the contract price without raising complaints. The court rejected the assertion that the supplier lacked capability based solely on an ACRA search conducted years later and held that there was no evidence of loss or improper exposure arising from this transaction.

The court evaluated the Communication Matrices and concluded that while certain approvals were formally required for business activities outside the territory, new business activities, and write-offs above specified thresholds, oral approvals by the Presidents of Asia Pacific Operations, Yanagawa and later Honda, satisfied or cured these requirements. It accepted that the matrices did not convert every procedural misstep into a repudiatory breach and noted that clause 10(c) of the Employment Contract contemplated warnings and repetition for violations of work regulations. Applying the standard of reasonable care and diligence from Scintronix and the principles on serious misconduct and repudiatory breach from Phosagro, the court held that Wong's conduct did not reach the threshold of gross default or misconduct or conduct grossly incompatible with his duties, and that any technical breaches of internal procedures were either rectified or non-prejudicial.

On the counterclaim, the court dismissed Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd's claim for approximately SGD 2.88 million, comprising the IC Debt and MOS outstanding amounts, because it found no actionable breach of fiduciary or contractual duties by Wong that caused those losses. On the lawfulness of the summary dismissal, the court concluded that Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd was not entitled to rely on clause 10(b), (h) or (i) to summarily dismiss Wong without notice or payment in lieu. It then applied the minimum legal obligation rule from Alexander Proudfoot and Gunton, as elaborated in Aldabe Fermin, to determine damages. The court reasoned that, given the audit findings and the employer's subsequent actions, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd would have chosen to terminate Wong's employment on 2017-12-21 by exercising its contractual right under clause 8.1 to pay three months' basic salary in lieu of notice rather than allowing the contract to run to 2018-03-31 or giving working notice. It therefore assessed Wong's entitlement on the basis of a lawful termination with three months' salary in lieu of notice and considered his claims for notice pay, end-of-term payment, variable bonus and salary in lieu of accrued leave accordingly. Overall, the court held that the summary dismissal was unlawful and that Wong was entitled to contractual remedies, while the defendants' allegations of misconduct and their counterclaim were not made out.

Metadata.Coram: Wong Sung Boon v Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd and another
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd is a company whose business initially centred on wholesale, repair and maintenance of office equipment described as print products.
Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd is owned by Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd, which in turn is owned by Fuji Xerox Co Ltd, a company jointly owned by Xerox Limited and Fujifilm Holdings Corporation.

Around 2001 to 2002, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd diversified its business to include providing financing, infrastructural support, project management services and selling products manufactured by third parties and non-Fuji Xerox-related entities.

In 2014-04, a media release stated that Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd would offer a full suite of financial solutions including hire purchase or leasing, sales and leaseback schemes, credit control outsourcing services and litigation services.

Public materials around 2014 described Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd and its Innovation Office in Singapore as operating consultancy-type services and expanding into wearable technology, telepresence, artificial intelligence and a cloud-based audio tour guide service.
By 2017-04-01, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd had renewed Wong's Employment Contract for the period from 2017-04-01 to 2018-03-31.

From 2017-04 to 2017-06, an Independent Investigation Committee established by Fujifilm conducted an investigation into accounting practices in Fuji Xerox Australia and Fuji Xerox New Zealand, subsidiaries of Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd.

In 2017-09, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd commissioned Deloitte & Touche Financial Advisory Services Pte Ltd to conduct an audit investigation into certain past transactions, with the investigation occur between 2017-09-13 and 2017-09-29.
On 2017-10-19, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd suspended its Senior Managing Director, Wong, with full pay pending the outcome of the Deloitte audit investigation.

On 2017-11-08, Deloitte & Touche Financial Advisory Services Pte Ltd issued a report to Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd concerning its audit investigation into past transactions involving I-Connect Interactive Pte Ltd and Supreme Lion Holding Pte Ltd.

On 2017-12-07, Fujifilm's Global Audit Division produced a special audit report that highlighted irregularities in Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd's transactions with I-Connect Interactive Pte Ltd, Supreme Lion Holding Pte Ltd and M.O.S. Marine Offshore Services Pte Ltd.

On 2011-10-11, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd entered into a Master Services Agreement with I-Connect Interactive Pte Ltd for supply, construction, installation and commissioning of base transceiver station equipment and facilities for a satellite infrastructure project in Indonesia for approximately SGD 4.3 million.

On 2012-03-16, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd entered into a Master Services Agreement with I-Connect Interactive Pte Ltd for supply, construction and installation of fibre optic infrastructure for a satellite infrastructure project in Indonesia for approximately SGD 5.59 million.
On 2011-11-01, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd's Legal Department signed the IC1 Contract with I-Connect Interactive Pte Ltd after internal review.
Around 2012-04-01, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd executed the IC2 Contract with I-Connect Interactive Pte Ltd after completion of internal credit evaluation and contract clearance.
By mid-2013, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd recorded an outstanding amount of SGD 557,921.50 due from I-Connect Interactive Pte Ltd under the IC2 Contract, internally referred to as the IC Debt.
Around 2014-06, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd internally approved the accounting write-off of the IC Debt of SGD 557,921.50 owed by I-Connect Interactive Pte Ltd.

In 2015, at its annual general meeting, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd's board and shareholders approved audited accounts for the financial year April 2014 to March 2015 that reflected the IC Debt as written off.

On 2014-03-05, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd entered into the MOS1 Contract with M.O.S. Marine Offshore Services Pte Ltd to supply offshore diving equipment and provide testing, commissioning and installation services for SGD 1,000,000.

On 2014-12-01, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd entered into the MOS2 Contract with M.O.S. Marine Offshore Services Pte Ltd to sell diving equipment and repair, refurbish and test a transformer and capacitor load bank for SGD 2,000,000.

On 2015-08-28, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd entered into the MOS3 Contract with M.O.S. Marine Offshore Services Pte Ltd to lease diving equipment and provide testing, commissioning and installation services for SGD 1,000,000.

On 2015-11-04, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd entered into the MOS4 Contract with M.O.S. Marine Offshore Services Pte Ltd to lease diving equipment and provide repair and reconditioning work on a transformer and capacitor load bank unit for SGD 1,007,496.
By 2016-01-01, M.O.S. Marine Offshore Services Pte Ltd had outstanding payment obligations to Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd under the MOS Contracts amounting to approximately SGD 2,322,464.28.

On 2013-12-06, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd entered into a sale agreement with Supreme Lion Holding Pte Ltd to supply products and services for installation, testing and commissioning of power control units for woodwork machinery systems.
By 2014-01-01, Supreme Lion Holding Pte Ltd had paid Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd the agreed contract price under the woodwork machinery systems sale agreement.

On 2011-10-01, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd operated under a 2006 Communication Matrix that required concurrence from the President of Asia Pacific Operations for certain business activities outside the territory and for write-offs of bad debts above specified thresholds.

On 2013-12-02, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd circulated a 2013 Communication Matrix by email to senior personnel, including Wong, setting out approval requirements for new business activities, unique products and write-offs of debts above specified amounts.

In 2015, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd issued an Operating Procedure Manual that formalised certain internal processes but did not specify a mandatory requirement to obtain personal guarantees for customer transactions.

On 2011-10-19, a credit evaluation form for the IC1 Contract with I-Connect Interactive Pte Ltd was submitted within Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd's credit evaluation process, which used scorecards and reports to assess customer creditworthiness.
On 2012-03-29, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd completed a credit evaluation form and related documents for the IC2 Contract with I-Connect Interactive Pte Ltd.

For the IC1 and IC2 Contracts, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd obtained personal guarantees from three individuals associated with I-Connect Interactive Pte Ltd, including directors and shareholders such as Raden and Suleman.

For the MOS2 Contract, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd obtained personal guarantees from three individuals associated with M.O.S. Marine Offshore Services Pte Ltd: director and shareholder Samuel Yeo, and individuals Rachael Yeo and Elizabeth Lee.

For the MOS3 and MOS4 Contracts, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd obtained personal guarantees from four individuals associated with M.O.S. Marine Offshore Services Pte Ltd: Samuel Yeo, Rachael Yeo, Elizabeth Lee and Adrian Lim.
Credit evaluation documents for the MOS2 Contract recorded that the combined annual income of personal guarantors Samuel and Rachael Yeo was approximately SGD 675,000 based on income tax assessments.

Credit evaluation documents for the MOS4 Contract recorded that the combined annual income of personal guarantors Samuel and Rachael Yeo was approximately SGD 1,460,000 based on updated income tax assessments.

In 2013-08, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd obtained search results showing that a bankruptcy application had been filed in 2013-07 against Suleman, one of the personal guarantors for I-Connect Interactive Pte Ltd.
By mid-2014, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd obtained search results indicating that Raden, a personal guarantor for I-Connect Interactive Pte Ltd, was working as a taxi driver.

On 2014-12-19, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd issued a purchase order to Serve Tech Container Specialist Pte Ltd for fabrication and installation works of diving equipment related to the MOS2 Contract, standard terms and conditions including warranties and indemnities.

On 2013-12-19, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd issued a purchase order to Hyper Marketing (Singapore) Pte Ltd for woodwork machinery system products and services related to the Supreme Lion transaction, standard terms and conditions including warranties and indemnities.

By 2014-01-01, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd had implemented an Abacus billing system that required contract descriptions to be selected from predefined categories such as rental agreements, influencing how some contracts were documented.

On 2017-12-21, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd issued a termination notice to Wong stating that it was terminating his Employment Contract with immediate effect pursuant to specified clauses and that he would not receive notice pay, compensation or an end-of-term payment.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on pre-dispute contractual terms, internal approval and credit processes, and the structure of the I-Connect, MOS and Supreme Lion transactions, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd's decision to terminate Wong's employment without notice or payment in lieu corresponded to conduct falling within the summary termination clauses of his Employment Contract or instead to a situation where only termination with notice or salary in lieu was contractually available.
Party_Details.Rule: An employer seeking to rely on contractual summary termination provisions must the employee's conduct falls within the defined categories such as causing material damage, gross default or misconduct, or conduct grossly incompatible with the due and faithful discharge of duties. Senior managers and directors are act with reasonable care, skill and diligence, to comply with internal approval matrices and credit procedures, and to manage transactional risk through appropriate documentation and oversight. Where summary termination is not justified, damages for dismissal are assessed under the minimum legal obligation principle, limiting the employer's liability to the least costly lawful method of terminating the contract, typically by exercising a contractual right to terminate on notice or with salary in lieu, and taking into account any contractual entitlements that would have accrued during that period.
Party_Details.Application: Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd operated within a corporate group structure under Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd and Fuji Xerox Co Ltd, with regional oversight by Presidents of Asia Pacific Operations. Its business evolved from print products into project management, third-party product sales and financial solutions, as reflected in public materials and internal mandates to grow revenue and expand scope. The company maintained Communication Matrices in 2006 and 2013 that required concurrence from the regional president for certain business activities and write-offs, and a credit evaluation process that used forms, scorecards and reports and allowed reliance on internal and public information when standard supporting documents were unavailable. During Wong's tenure, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd entered into the IC1 and IC2 Contracts with I-Connect Interactive Pte Ltd, the MOS1–MOS4 Contracts with M.O.S. Marine Offshore Services Pte Ltd, and the Supreme Lion transaction, with its Legal Department clearing key contracts and its Finance and Credit teams conducting evaluations and obtaining personal guarantees. The company contracted directly with suppliers under purchase orders incorporating standard terms, warranties and indemnities, and customers such as Supreme Lion and MOS initially made substantial payments. The IC Debt was later written off and reflected in audited accounts approved by the board and the regional president. In 2017, following group-level investigations and audit reports highlighting irregularities, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd suspended Wong and then issued a termination notice on 2017-12-21 invoking summary termination clauses and denying notice pay and end-of-term payment. Given the existence of a contractual mechanism to terminate by paying three months' basic salary in lieu of notice and the ability to allow the fixed term to expire, the company's minimum legal obligation at that time was to choose the least costly lawful termination method. The pre-dispute record shows that internal approvals could be and were given orally by regional presidents, that the Legal Department and Finance staff participated in structuring and clearing the transactions, and that the company had the option to address any transactional concerns through internal governance and civil claims while still complying with the contractual notice framework. The decision to proceed by summary dismissal without notice pay therefore exposed the company to the risk that a court would later characterise the termination as unlawful and limit its position to what it could have achieved by exercising the notice provisions.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The court considered Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd's reliance on clauses 10(b), (h) and (i) of Wong's Employment Contract to justify summary dismissal. It examined the company's internal governance framework, including the 2006 and 2013 Communication Matrices and the credit evaluation process, and the factual circumstances of the I-Connect, MOS and Supreme Lion transactions. The court found that the IC1 and IC2 Contracts were project management-type arrangements with back-to-back supplier contracts and that the Tsalasa Contract contained warranties and undertakings protecting Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd. It noted that I-Connect did not allege breach and that suppliers performed their obligations. The court accepted evidence that Wong consulted relevant departments, that the Legal Department cleared the IC1 Contract, and that the then President of Asia Pacific Operations, Yanagawa, orally approved the IC1 and IC2 Contracts at a Business Review Meeting, thereby satisfying or curing any Communication Matrix approval requirement. It held that although Wong did not initially obtain formal approval before entering the IC1 Contract or before writing off the IC Debt, subsequent approval by the regional president and the board, including Honda's acceptance of the audited accounts reflecting the write-off, rectified any procedural breach. The court concluded that these matters did not cause material damage to Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd and did not amount to conduct grossly incompatible with Wong's duties or to gross default or misconduct under clause 10(b), (h) or (i).

For the MOS transactions, the court rejected Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd's characterisation of the MOS2, MOS3 and MOS4 Contracts as pure loans disguised as sales or rentals. It relied on the contractual documents, supplier quotations and purchase orders, manual costing sheets, and testimony from finance staff to find that the MOS2 Contract was an instalment sale with a gross profit margin and that the MOS3 and MOS4 Contracts, though documented as rental agreements due to limitations of the Abacus billing system, were in substance asset sale or lease transactions with underlying goods and services supplied by Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd through its suppliers. The court noted that Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd contracted directly with suppliers, that goods were delivered and no complaints were raised by MOS, and that the Legal Department had cleared the MOS2 Contract. It accepted that Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd was already providing commercial financing and project management services and that the MOS transactions fell within an expanded business model encouraged by regional management. The court found that Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd obtained multiple personal guarantees from individuals associated with MOS, that detailed credit evaluations were performed, and that MOS had a positive payment history before later default. It held that the company failed to show that Wong exposed it to unreasonable or unnecessary liability or that he failed to exercise reasonable diligence.

Regarding the Supreme Lion transaction, the court found that Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd entered into a sale agreement to supply woodwork machinery system products and services, that it contracted with Hyper Marketing (Singapore) Pte Ltd under purchase orders incorporating standard terms, warranties and indemnities, and that Supreme Lion paid the contract price without raising complaints. The court rejected the assertion that the supplier lacked capability based solely on an ACRA search conducted years later and held that there was no evidence of loss or improper exposure arising from this transaction.

The court evaluated the Communication Matrices and concluded that while certain approvals were formally required for business activities outside the territory, new business activities, and write-offs above specified thresholds, oral approvals by the Presidents of Asia Pacific Operations, Yanagawa and later Honda, satisfied or cured these requirements. It accepted that the matrices did not convert every procedural misstep into a repudiatory breach and noted that clause 10(c) of the Employment Contract contemplated warnings and repetition for violations of work regulations. Applying the standard of reasonable care and diligence from Scintronix and the principles on serious misconduct and repudiatory breach from Phosagro, the court held that Wong's conduct did not reach the threshold of gross default or misconduct or conduct grossly incompatible with his duties, and that any technical breaches of internal procedures were either rectified or non-prejudicial.

On Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd's counterclaim for approximately SGD 2.88 million, the court held that the company had not established that Wong's conduct caused the IC Debt or the MOS outstanding amounts in a way that would ground liability for breach of fiduciary or contractual duties. It therefore dismissed the counterclaim. On the lawfulness of the summary dismissal, the court concluded that Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd was not entitled to rely on clause 10(b), (h) or (i) to summarily dismiss Wong without notice or payment in lieu. It then applied the minimum legal obligation rule from Alexander Proudfoot and Gunton, as elaborated in Aldabe Fermin, to determine damages. The court reasoned that, given the audit findings and the employer's subsequent actions, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd would have chosen to terminate Wong's employment on 2017-12-21 by exercising its contractual right under clause 8.1 to pay three months' basic salary in lieu of notice rather than allowing the contract to run to 2018-03-31 or giving working notice. It therefore assessed Wong's entitlement on the basis of a lawful termination with three months' salary in lieu of notice and considered his claims for notice pay, end-of-term payment, variable bonus and salary in lieu of accrued leave accordingly. The court held that the summary dismissal was unlawful and that Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd was liable for wrongful dismissal within the limits of the minimum legal obligation framework.

Metadata.Coram: Wong Sung Boon v Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd and another
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd is a company that owns Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd and is itself owned by Fuji Xerox Co Ltd.
From around 2016-07-01, Katsumi Kizaki served as General Manager of the Legal Department of Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd.
At material times, the President of Asia Pacific Operations also held the position of President of Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd and was a director of Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd.

From 2017-04 to 2017-06, an Independent Investigation Committee established by Fujifilm conducted an investigation into accounting practices in Fuji Xerox Australia and Fuji Xerox New Zealand, subsidiaries of Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd.

Around 2017-09-01, Fujifilm's Global Audit Division commenced investigations into allegations of serious misconduct relating to Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd, in parallel with an audit investigation by Deloitte & Touche Financial Advisory Services Pte Ltd.

On 2017-12-07, Fujifilm's Global Audit Division presented a special audit report to Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd and related entities, highlighting irregularities in transactions involving Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd and counterparties I-Connect Interactive Pte Ltd, Supreme Lion Holding Pte Ltd and M.O.S. Marine Offshore Services Pte Ltd.

In early 2012, during a Business Review Meeting, the then President of Asia Pacific Operations and President of Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd, Katsuhiko Yanagawa, was informed by Wong that Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd had entered into the IC1 Contract and intended to enter into the IC2 Contract with I-Connect Interactive Pte Ltd.

In early 2014, during a Business Review Meeting, the then President of Asia Pacific Operations and President of Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd, Masashi Honda, was informed by Wong that Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd was considering transactions with M.O.S. Marine Offshore Services Pte Ltd involving diving equipment.

In 2015, as President of Asia Pacific Operations and President of Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd, Masashi Honda attended the annual general meeting of Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd and signed off on its audited accounts for the financial year April 2014 to March 2015, which reflected the write-off of the IC Debt.

On 2013-12-02, Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd circulated the 2013 Communication Matrix by email to senior personnel in group companies, including Wong, setting out approval requirements for new business activities, unique products and write-offs of debts above specified amounts.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on pre-dispute group governance structures and communications, Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd's involvement in oversight, audit and approval processes relating to Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd's transactions and Wong's employment could support a claim that it participated in or induced a contractual in Wong's summary termination, or whether its role remained within group-level oversight and audit functions.
Party_Details.Rule: A parent or regional holding company may be in conspiracy or for inducing of contract if it intentionally participates in a combination to cause unlawful injury or knowingly and intentionally procures a subsidiary's of contract without justification. Group-level oversight, audit activities and approval of subsidiary accounts, when carried out within the scope of corporate governance, do not by themselves constitute inducement of . The existence of internal communication matrices and audit reports may inform whether the parent had knowledge of relevant facts, but liability requires proof of intentional procurement of a contractual rather than mere exercise of oversight.
Party_Details.Application: Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd owned Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd and provided regional oversight and direction, including through the office of the President of Asia Pacific Operations, who also served as President of Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd and as a director of Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd. The company participated in group-level investigations by Fujifilm's Independent Investigation Committee and Global Audit Division, which examined accounting practices in regional subsidiaries and produced reports in 2017 highlighting irregularities in certain transactions involving Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd. Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd circulated the 2013 Communication Matrix to senior personnel, including Wong, and its presidents attended Business Review Meetings where Wong described proposed and existing transactions such as the I-Connect and MOS projects. The presidents also attended Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd's annual general meetings and approved audited accounts that included the write-off of the IC Debt. These activities Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd exercised oversight and governance functions, including setting approval frameworks and reviewing subsidiary accounts. The pre-dispute record does not Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd directly instructed Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd to terminate Wong's Employment Contract in a manner inconsistent with its terms, nor that it directed the subsidiary to withhold notice pay or end-of-term payments. Instead, the termination decision was implemented by Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd through a termination notice invoking clauses of Wong's Employment Contract. Within this framework, Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd's role appears confined to oversight, audit and approval functions rather than explicit procurement of a contractual .
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The court considered Wong's claims that Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd conspired with Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd to injure him by dismissal or wrongfully induced Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd to breach his Employment Contract. It examined the corporate structure, noting that Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd owned Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd and that the Presidents of Asia Pacific Operations, Yanagawa and later Honda, held positions in both companies and sat on the board of Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd. The court reviewed the evidence of Business Review Meetings at which Wong presented the I-Connect and MOS transactions to the regional presidents, and the circulation of the 2013 Communication Matrix by Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd to senior personnel, including Wong. It also noted that Honda, as regional president and director, attended the annual general meeting of Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd and signed off on audited accounts reflecting the write-off of the IC Debt.

The court accepted that Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd, through its presidents and legal department, exercised oversight and governance over Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd, including setting approval frameworks and participating in group-level investigations and audits. However, it found no evidence that Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd, as a separate legal entity, had directed or procured Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd to terminate Wong's Employment Contract in breach of its terms. The termination notice was issued by Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd and relied on clauses in Wong's Employment Contract. The court held that group-level audit findings and oversight, including the Deloitte Report and the Fujifilm Global Audit Division special audit report, did not by themselves amount to inducement of breach or conspiracy. It concluded that Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd's actions were consistent with its role in corporate governance and did not satisfy the elements of conspiracy or inducement of breach of contract. Accordingly, the claims against Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd were dismissed and it was not held liable for Wong's unlawful dismissal or for any of the alleged transactional losses.
